description: Expanded Canadian contracts pack for doctrinal retrieval, law-school-style study, and assistant support. Covers formation, interpretation, performance, breach, remedies, equitable doctrines, consumer and standard-form issues, relational contracting, good faith, and licensing / exam-oriented issue spotting. Educational only — not legal advice.

topics.title: Formation: Offer and Acceptance
topics.coreDefinition: A legally binding contract requires a clear offer, an unequivocal acceptance, consideration, and intention to create legal relations.
frameworkSteps: Identify a clear offer with definite terms.
Confirm acceptance that mirrors the offer (no material variation).
Verify consideration (something of value exchanged).
Assess intention to create legal relations.
quickExamples: A signed service agreement outlining scope and payment.
An email confirming acceptance of quoted terms.
commonMistakes: Confusing negotiations with a final offer.
Assuming informal discussions automatically create binding agreements.

topics.title: Consideration
topics.coreDefinition: Consideration requires that each party provide something of value. Past consideration generally does not qualify.
frameworkSteps: Identify what each party is promising or providing.
Determine whether exchange is legally sufficient.
Assess whether modification requires fresh consideration.
quickExamples: Payment in exchange for services.
Promise to deliver goods in exchange for payment.
commonMistakes: Assuming moral obligation equals legal consideration.
Believing contract modifications are valid without new consideration.

topics.title: Intention and Capacity
topics.coreDefinition: Parties must intend legal consequences and possess legal capacity to contract.
frameworkSteps: Assess whether context implies legal intent (commercial vs social setting).
Confirm parties have legal capacity (age, mental competence).
Evaluate whether any incapacity renders agreement voidable.
quickExamples: Commercial agreements presume legal intent.
Contracts with minors may be unenforceable in some contexts.
commonMistakes: Assuming all written agreements are automatically enforceable.
Ignoring capacity concerns.

topics.title: Contract Interpretation
topics.coreDefinition: Contracts are interpreted contextually, giving words their ordinary meaning within the entire agreement and surrounding circumstances.
frameworkSteps: Read entire contract as a whole.
Consider factual matrix (background context).
Resolve ambiguities using standard interpretive principles.
quickExamples: Ambiguous clause interpreted against drafter (contra proferentem).
commonMistakes: Isolating one clause without reading entire agreement.
Ignoring context at time of formation.

topics.title: Breach of Contract
topics.coreDefinition: A breach occurs when a party fails to perform contractual obligations without lawful excuse.
frameworkSteps: Identify contractual obligation.
Determine whether performance failed or was deficient.
Assess materiality of breach.
Consider available remedies.
quickExamples: Failure to deliver goods on agreed date.
Failure to pay agreed compensation.
commonMistakes: Assuming minor deviation automatically voids contract.
Failing to review termination clauses.

topics.title: Damages and Remedies
topics.coreDefinition: Damages aim to place the injured party in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed.
frameworkSteps: Assess direct losses.
Evaluate foreseeability (Hadley principle).
Consider mitigation obligations.
Review contractual limitation clauses.
quickExamples: Lost profits reasonably foreseeable at time of contract.
Duty to mitigate loss by seeking substitute contract.
commonMistakes: Claiming speculative damages.
Ignoring mitigation requirement.

topics.title: Common Contract Defences
topics.coreDefinition: Certain circumstances may render contracts void, voidable, or unenforceable.
frameworkSteps: Identify alleged defect (misrepresentation, mistake, duress, undue influence).
Determine whether defence voids or makes contract voidable.
Assess evidence supporting defence.
quickExamples: Material misrepresentation may allow rescission.
Duress may invalidate agreement.
commonMistakes: Confusing unfair bargain with legal duress.
Assuming verbal misstatements automatically void agreement.

intentSignals: can I get out of this contract

topics.title: Good Faith and Honest Performance
topics.coreDefinition: Canadian contract law recognizes an organizing principle of good faith and a general duty of honest performance. Parties must not lie or knowingly mislead each other about contractual performance, and certain discretionary powers must be exercised in a manner consistent with the bargain.
frameworkSteps: Identify the contractual power or performance obligation in dispute.
Ask whether the conduct involved dishonesty, misleading performance communication, or abuse of discretion.
Distinguish ordinary self-interest from conduct that defeats the contract's legitimate expectations.
Assess remedies and causation tied to the dishonest or bad-faith conduct.
quickExamples: A party knowingly misleads the counterparty about whether a termination right will be exercised.
A discretionary pricing or allocation power is used in a way that undermines the contractual bargain.
commonMistakes: Treating good faith as a free-floating fairness test untethered to the contract.
Assuming every sharp practice is automatically actionable without doctrinal fit.

intentSignals: did they act in bad faith
can they do this even if contract says so

leadingCases.name: Bhasin v Hrynew
leadingCases.principle: Organizing principle of good faith; duty of honest performance.

leadingCases.name: C.M. Callow Inc. v Zollinger
leadingCases.principle: Misleading exercise of termination rights and honest performance.

leadingCases.name: Wastech Services Ltd. v Greater Vancouver Sewerage and Drainage District

topics.title: Standard Form Contracts, Inequality of Bargaining Power, and Unconscionability
topics.coreDefinition: Standard-form and non-negotiated agreements may be enforceable, but courts scrutinize clauses affected by unfair surprise, grossly improvident bargains, inequality of bargaining power, or public-policy concerns.
frameworkSteps: Determine whether the contract was standard form, clickwrap, consumer-facing, or mandatory for access to work/services.
Assess bargaining inequality, practical ability to negotiate, and cost barriers to enforcement.
Review arbitration, forum-selection, exclusion, and notice clauses for enforceability.
Consider statutory overlays such as consumer-protection legislation.
quickExamples: A mandatory arbitration clause makes small-value claims realistically impossible to pursue.
A buried exclusion clause is inconsistent with reasonable notice or statutory consumer rights.
commonMistakes: Assuming digital assent makes every clause immune from review.
Ignoring provincial consumer-protection statutes.

intentSignals: do i have to accept this

leadingCases.name: Douez v Facebook, Inc.
leadingCases.principle: Forum-selection clause scrutiny in consumer/privacy context.

leadingCases.name: Tercon Contractors Ltd. v British Columbia (Transportation and Highways)

topics.title: Privity, Assignment, and Third-Party Effects
topics.coreDefinition: Contracts generally bind the parties who made them, but rights may sometimes be assigned and third parties may benefit or be affected depending on doctrine, wording, and statute.
frameworkSteps: Identify who is party to the contract and who is attempting to enforce or rely on it.
Distinguish assignment of rights from delegation of performance.
Check whether the contract restricts assignment or requires consent.
Consider recognized exceptions and contextual doctrines involving third-party beneficiaries.
quickExamples: A receivables payment right is assigned to a lender.
A subcontractor seeks to rely on a limitation clause in a prime contract.
commonMistakes: Confusing assignment of benefits with transfer of obligations.
Assuming a helpful clause is automatically enforceable by any related entity.

topics.title: Conditions, Warranties, Innominate Terms, and Termination
topics.coreDefinition: Not every breach permits termination. Courts distinguish between more fundamental obligations and less central promises, often asking whether the breach substantially deprives the innocent party of the expected benefit.
frameworkSteps: Identify the breached term and its role in the bargain.
Assess seriousness, timing, and commercial consequences of the breach.
Review express termination, cure, and notice clauses.
Separate right to terminate from right to damages.
quickExamples: Late delivery in a seasonal supply contract may be fundamental if timing was central.
A minor technical breach may support damages but not termination.
commonMistakes: Assuming any breach allows immediate exit.
Ignoring contractual notice and cure mechanisms.

intentSignals: is this serious enough to terminate

topics.title: Equitable Relief: Specific Performance, Rectification, and Rescission
topics.coreDefinition: Equitable remedies may be available where damages are inadequate or where the written instrument does not reflect the parties' true agreement, subject to fairness and evidentiary limits.
frameworkSteps: Identify why monetary damages may be inadequate.
Assess whether the remedy sought is prospective performance, correction of a written instrument, or unwinding of the transaction.
Check conduct-based bars such as delay, clean hands, or practical impossibility.
Tie the equitable remedy to evidence of the actual bargain and the relief truly needed.
quickExamples: Unique land transaction where damages do not fully compensate.
Drafting error causes the written clause to fail to reflect the shared agreement.
commonMistakes: Treating specific performance as routine.
Seeking rectification without strong evidence of a prior definite consensus.

topics.title: Contract Drafting Architecture
topics.coreDefinition: Transactional analysis depends on how the contract is built: parties, definitions, operative obligations, payment mechanics, risk allocation, change control, term/termination, dispute clauses, and schedules.
frameworkSteps: Map the contract into structural blocks before analyzing individual clauses.
Identify whether the agreement is product, services, license, employment, financing, settlement, or mixed.
Test internal consistency across defined terms, schedules, and remedies.
Look for silent risk points: IP ownership, data use, limits of liability, renewal, assignment, governing law, notice.
quickExamples: A SaaS agreement needs service levels, data handling, IP allocation, and suspension/termination logic.
A consulting MSA needs statement-of-work hierarchy and change-order mechanics.
commonMistakes: Reading clauses in isolation without agreement hierarchy.
Ignoring schedules, exhibits, and order-of-precedence language.

intentSignals: what should be in this contract

modules.title: 1L Contract Formation and Enforceability
learningOutcomes: Spot whether negotiations have crystallized into an enforceable agreement.
Separate preliminary negotiation language from definite contractual commitment.
Explain how consideration and intention operate in commercial settings.

modules.title: 1L/2L Interpretation and Performance
learningOutcomes: Interpret clauses contextually without collapsing into free-form fairness review.
Distinguish textual ambiguity from dissatisfaction with the deal.
Connect interpretive disputes to remedy questions.

modules.title: 2L Remedies and Equitable Control
learningOutcomes: Choose the remedy that fits the injury and the contract structure.
Explain foreseeability and mitigation in concrete factual settings.
Differentiate equitable relief from damages and tie each to evidence.

modules.title: Upper-Year Commercial and Standard-Form Problems
learningOutcomes: Assess enforceability of standard-form clauses under common law and statute.
Recognize bargaining-power problems in digital and platform contracts.

modules.title: Licensing / Bar and NCA Issue Spotting
learningOutcomes: Answer open-book or issue-heavy prompts with structure instead of rule-dumping.
Move from doctrinal recall to problem solving and client-facing explanation.
Integrate contracts doctrine with professional responsibility and practice management.

caseStudies.title: Founder SaaS Master Services Agreement Breakdown
caseStudies.scenario: A startup signs a rushed SaaS MSA with auto-renewal, broad data-use language, a one-way limitation of liability, and ambiguous service-level commitments. The relationship sours after repeated outages.
issuesToSpot: Liability cap carve-outs and data/security breaches

caseStudies.title: Platform Arbitration Clause and Access-to-Justice Problem
caseStudies.scenario: A user must accept a standard-form digital contract with a foreign arbitration clause and high upfront costs to challenge a small-value dispute.
recommendedOutputs: case comparison to Uber and Douez

caseStudies.title: Distributor Termination After Reassurances
caseStudies.scenario: A manufacturer reassures a distributor that the relationship is stable while internally planning a non-renewal that destroys the distributor's near-term business planning.

caseStudies.title: Share Purchase Agreement and Misrepresentation
caseStudies.scenario: A buyer alleges the seller concealed key liabilities before signing a share purchase agreement containing representations, warranties, indemnities, and an entire-agreement clause.

caseStudies.title: Construction Scope Creep and Change Orders
caseStudies.scenario: A contractor performs additional work after informal site instructions, but the contract requires signed written change orders and strict payment certification.

courtCases.name: Sattva Capital Corp. v Creston Moly Corp.
courtCases.whyItMatters: Contextual approach to contractual interpretation and appellate restraint.

courtCases.name: Bhasin v Hrynew
courtCases.whyItMatters: Recognizes the organizing principle of good faith and the duty of honest performance.

courtCases.name: Tercon Contractors Ltd. v British Columbia (Transportation and Highways)
courtCases.whyItMatters: Leading modern framework for exclusion-clause enforceability.

courtCases.name: Uber Technologies Inc. v Heller
courtCases.whyItMatters: Modern treatment of unconscionability and inaccessible arbitration in standard-form contracts.

courtCases.name: Douez v Facebook, Inc.
courtCases.whyItMatters: Consumer/privacy context and limits of forum-selection enforcement.

courtCases.name: Wastech Services Ltd. v Greater Vancouver Sewerage and Drainage District
Matthews v Ocean Nutrition Canada Ltd.
courtCases.whyItMatters: Contractual damages analysis in the employment-related incentive context.

contractArchetypes.name: Master Services Agreement + Statement of Work

projects.title: Clause Risk Heatmap
projects.goal: Build a repeatable system for grading clause risk across limitation, indemnity, termination, dispute resolution, assignment, and IP terms.

projects.title: Open-Book Contracts Index
projects.goal: Create a contracts doctrine and case index optimized for licensing-style exams and time-constrained issue spotting.

projects.title: Contract Lifecycle Evidence Pack
projects.goal: Map what documents to preserve from negotiation through termination for later disputes or audits.

projects.title: Nyx Contract Explainer Layer
projects.goal: Translate doctrinal contract issues into short, accurate, non-advice explanations with clarifier prompts.

useCases: plain-language explanation of whether an agreement likely formed
clause red-flag explanations for founders and operators
issue-spotting prompts before the user talks to counsel
responseRules: Lead with contract structure before discussing outcomes.
Use conditional language; avoid guaranteeing enforceability.
Ask for jurisdiction when procedure, limitation periods, or statutory consumer overlays matter.

useCases: comparison of remedies and drafting choices
building research paths from source hierarchy
reasoningPatterns: text -> context -> doctrine -> evidence -> practical lane
common law doctrine plus statutory overlay where relevant

treatisesAndTextbooks.title: Swan, Adamski & Na, Canadian Contract Law
treatisesAndTextbooks.use: core doctrinal anchor for formation, interpretation, remedies, and defences

treatisesAndTextbooks.title: Fridman, The Law of Contract in Canada
McCamus, The Law of Contracts
treatisesAndTextbooks.use: good-faith, interpretation, and broader common-law analysis

treatisesAndTextbooks.title: Ben-Ishai, Percy & Boyle, Contracts: Cases and Commentaries
treatisesAndTextbooks.use: law-school style issue spotting and doctrinal synthesis

openOrOfficialSources.type: public case law and legislation repository
openOrOfficialSources.use: case retrieval, noting up support, comparative jurisdiction scanning

openOrOfficialSources.title: Ontario e-Laws
openOrOfficialSources.use: Ontario statutory overlays for business / consumer contexts

examAndCurriculumAnchors.title: NCA Assigned Requirements and Exam Outlines
examAndCurriculumAnchors.use: Canadian common-law doctrinal baseline and study expectations

notes: Designed for structured educational analysis, not legal advice.
Canadian orientation with province-specific overlays often required for procedure, consumer law, and limitations.
Textbook references are bibliographic anchors only; no copyrighted chapters reproduced.
